description:
You are a security engineer who has just completed a round of credential rotation.  
A colleague has asked for a quick, one-off report that lists every log entry where an **old password hash** was still present in the rotation logs, so they can double-check that no secrets were accidentally written to disk.

Environment
1. A directory already exists at  
   /home/user/rotation
2. Inside that directory there is a text file called  
   /home/user/rotation/credential_rotation.log  
   The file is UTF-8 encoded, contains fewer than 100 lines and uses the following fixed log format (one line per event):  
        [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] user=<username> action=<verb> …  
   • Lines that contain an old password have the token  
        password_old=<hex-hash>  
     somewhere after the action field.  
   • Other lines (e.g. “confirm” events) never contain the string “password_old=”.

Task
1. With a **single terminal command** that may combine standard GNU/Linux text-processing utilities (e.g. grep, sed, awk, cut, perl, etc.), filter credential_rotation.log so that only the lines containing the literal text “password_old=” remain.  
2. From those lines extract exactly two fields in the following order and write them to a new file:  
   a. The timestamp in the form YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (no brackets)  
   b. The username (the value that follows “user=”)  
3. Separate the two fields with a single horizontal tab character (`\t`).  
4. Preserve the chronological order in which the matching lines appear in the original log.  
5. Save the resulting three-line* report to  
   /home/user/rotation/leaked_old_passwords.tsv  
6. After creating the file, print its entire contents to STDOUT so that a human reviewer can immediately see the result.

Output format example
If the source log contained a line:
    [2024-01-02 03:04:05] user=jane action=update password_old=deadbeef …
then the corresponding line in leaked_old_passwords.tsv must be:
    2024-01-02 03:04:05<tab>jane

Grading notes
• The automated tests will verify that leaked_old_passwords.tsv exists at the specified path.  
• The file must contain exactly three lines, each ending with a UNIX newline (`\n`).  
• Each line must consist of a timestamp, a single “\t” character, and a username—nothing more.  
• Ordering must match the chronological order of the original log entries.

*The current log happens to contain exactly three “password_old=” events, so your output file will also contain exactly three lines.

Hint: think about combining a positive grep (to keep only the lines of interest) with a tiny bit of awk or sed to pull out the two required fields, separated by “\t”.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (before the agent starts):
1. Directory:
   /home/user/rotation
2. File:
   /home/user/rotation/credential_rotation.log  
   Exact contents (7 lines, each terminated with `\n`):

[2024-05-03 09:14:02] user=alice action=update password_old=2c1743a password_new=10ac5bd
[2024-05-03 09:15:45] user=alice action=confirm rotation_status=success
[2024-05-03 10:01:11] user=bob action=update password_old=ffab23a password_new=946adfe
[2024-05-03 10:03:02] user=bob action=confirm rotation_status=success
[2024-05-03 11:20:55] user=charlie action=update password_old=ab41dfe password_new=dfee551
[2024-05-03 11:22:11] user=charlie action=confirm rotation_status=success
[2024-05-03 12:10:13] user=david action=confirm rotation_status=success

Expected artefact after successful task completion:
File: /home/user/rotation/leaked_old_passwords.tsv  
Expected contents (including final newline):

2024-05-03 09:14:02\talice
2024-05-03 10:01:11\tbob
2024-05-03 11:20:55\tcharlie
name: task_000000_4c08a553
